post.title: The Myth of Social Hierarchies
post.body:
Just a random thought, but I think so-called social hierarchies in friend groups mostly come from people who simply put in more effort than others.

For example, I have a friend group where there's one person who's incredibly friendly toward everyone. Everyone feels close to them. I don't think that's because they're "popular" or because they're playing some sort of power game. I think it's simply because they genuinely put in the effort to build relationships with everyone.

Friend groups aren't naturally equal. You're never going to be equally close with every single person. Some people will naturally be closer to you than others.

But if you intentionally put in the effort to get to know everyone—to become close enough that each person considers you a close friend—you naturally become one of the core people in the group.

You don't even have to be equally close with everyone. You just have to build meaningful relationships with enough people.

Eventually, you become one of the people who ties the group together.

If you leave, conversations might feel awkward because you're the bridge between multiple people.

That made me realize something.

When someone worries, "I'm always ignored in this friend group,"—which, I'll admit, I used to do when I was younger—it might simply be because they haven't put enough effort into getting to know everyone individually.

Of course, there's another possibility.

Maybe you did put in the effort, and it just didn't work out.

In that case, maybe the group just isn't for you.

I've been in quite a few friend groups throughout my life, and I've realized that it depends heavily on the people.

You can't force yourself into becoming the "center" of every social group because sometimes you simply don't fit.

I think it's similar to cooking.

Imagine you're really confident in your ability to cook a steak. You make one for someone, and they don't like it.

You have two ways to react.

The first is to think,

"Oh my God, they didn't like my steak. My self-esteem is ruined. What am I doing wrong?"

The second is,

"I know my steak is good. I like how I make it. They just don't like it, and that's okay. It's a matter of preference."

That's self-esteem.

You know your own value, so you don't let one person's opinion completely define you.

I think friend groups work the same way.

Sometimes you don't need to change yourself just to fit in.

Sometimes the group simply isn't your crowd.

I actually got really lucky.

My childhood friend group never really felt like my people.

I don't think it was anyone's fault.

They weren't bad people.

I just don't think we were the right fit for each other.

When I was younger, though, I didn't realize that. I just thought, "These are the people I'm always around, so this must be where I belong."

Because of that, I constantly felt like I was at the bottom of the social hierarchy.

Then I moved to a different school.

Almost immediately, I found people I clicked with.

Those feelings mostly disappeared.

That experience taught me that sometimes it isn't about becoming more likable.

Sometimes it's simply about finding the right people.

This whole thought actually came after watching one of Jeff's videos about friend groups.

It made me realize that the people who become the center of a group usually do one of three things.

Either they naturally connect with everyone because they're very charismatic...

They intentionally put in the effort to become close with everyone...

Or they realize a particular group isn't for them and find one where they fit better.

Personally, I'm not charismatic enough to instantly become friends with everyone.

I know people who can walk into a room and naturally connect with almost anybody.

That's not me.

If I don't consciously put effort into socializing, I'll usually just feed off everyone else's energy instead of leading conversations.

But when I do want to become more involved in a group, I've found that the best thing to do is simply spend time getting to know everyone individually.

I actually did this once.

For my graduation party, I invited a bunch of friends who didn't know each other.

Over time, they all became friends.

Since everyone originally knew me before they knew each other, I naturally became one of the central people in that group.

Interestingly, though, that didn't last forever.

Shortly afterward, another friend became the one who hosted the most hangouts.

They were constantly reaching out to people and planning things.

Naturally, they became one of the core people in the group.

I saw the same thing happen in one of my university friend groups.

The group was really active for a while, but then some drama happened and everything slowly died down.

One friend, though, kept putting in the effort.

He suggested we go around rating pizza places.

Then that turned into regular hangouts.

Because he kept reaching out and organizing things, he naturally became one of the central people in the group.

It wasn't because everyone thought he was "popular."

I honestly think that idea of popularity is a very elementary-school way of looking at friendships.

The people we often think of as "popular" are usually just the people who are willing to put in the effort.

They're either naturally charismatic and can connect with almost anyone...

Or they're the ones constantly reaching out, planning hangouts, and maintaining friendships.

So if you're struggling to fit into a friend group, I think there are two questions worth asking yourself.

First, have you actually put in the effort to get to know everyone individually and help maintain the group?

And second, if you have, is this group simply not the right fit for you?

Sometimes the answer is the first.

Sometimes it's the second.

And that's okay.

Anyway, those are just my thoughts.

See you.
